markdown:
# Build. Run. Manage. Agentic Software at Scale
[ Explore the SDK and build your first Agent.](https://docs.agno.com/introduction)[ Turn your Agents into a production API.](https://docs.agno.com/agent-os/introduction)[ Production patterns for deploying your AgentOS.](https://docs.agno.com/production/overview)

```
 agno.agent  Agent
 agno.models.anthropic  Claude
 agno.tools.coding  CodingTools

agent = Agent(
    model=Claude(id=),
    tools=[CodingTools()],
    add_history_to_context=,
    markdown=,
)

agent.print_response("Build a todo app with tests.", stream=)
```



© 2025 Agno Inc. All rights reserved.
Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

To configure your environment for applications, you may need to set environment variables. This guide provides instructions for setting environment variables in both macOS (Shell) and Windows (PowerShell and Windows Command Prompt).
###  Setting Environment Variables in Shell
These environment variables will only be available in the current shell session. To display the environment variable: To make environment variables persist across sessions, add them to your shell configuration file (e.g., , , ). To display the environment variable:
###  Setting Environment Variables in PowerShell
These environment variables will only be available in the current PowerShell session. To display the environment variable: To make environment variables persist across sessions, add them to your PowerShell profile script (e.g., ). Add the following line to the profile script: Save and close the file, then reload the profile: To display the environment variable:
###  Setting Environment Variables in Windows Command Prompt
These environment variables will only be available in the current Command Prompt session. To display the environment variable: To make environment variables persist across sessions, you can use the command: Note: After setting an environment variable using , you need to restart the Command Prompt or any applications that need to read the new environment variable. To display the environment variable in a new Command Prompt session: By following these steps, you can effectively set and display environment variables in macOS Shell, Windows Command Prompt, and PowerShell. This will ensure your environment is properly configured for your applications.
Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

* 





A Workflow orchestrates agents, teams, and functions as a collection of steps. Steps can run sequentially, in parallel, in loops, or conditionally based on results. Output from each step flows to the next, creating a predictable pipeline for complex tasks. Here’s a simple workflow that takes a topic, researches it, and writes an article:

```






"Find relevant information about the topic"





"Write a clear, engaging article based on the research"







"Write an article about AI trends"

```

  * You need predictable, repeatable execution
  * Tasks have clear sequential steps with defined inputs and outputs
  * You want audit trails and consistent results across runs

Use a when you need flexible, collaborative problem-solving where agents coordinate dynamically.
##  What Can Be a Step?  
| Individual AI executor with specific tools and instructions  |  
| --- |  
| Coordinated group of agents for complex sub-tasks  |  
| Custom Python function for specialized logic  |  
| Nested workflow for composable, reusable sub-pipelines  |  
Workflows support conditional logic, parallel execution, loops, and conversational interactions. See the guides below for details.
[ Define steps, inputs, and outputs. ](https://docs.agno.com/workflows/building-workflows)
[ Execute workflows and handle responses. ](https://docs.agno.com/workflows/running-workflows)
[ Enable chat interactions on your workflows. ](https://docs.agno.com/workflows/conversational-workflows)
Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

In this guide, you’ll build an agent that:
  * Connects to an MCP server
  * Stores and retrieves past conversations
  * Runs as a production API

All in about 20 lines of code. Save the following code as :

```










# Agno docs via MCP






# Serve via AgentOS → streaming, auth, session isolation, API endpoints



```

  * Tracing enabled out of the box


Set up your virtual environment
Export your Anthropic API key
Your AgentOS is now running at:API documentation is automatically available at:
You can add your own routes, middleware, or any FastAPI feature on top.
##  3. Connect to the AgentOS UI
The connects directly from your browser to your runtime. It lets you test, monitor, and manage your agents in real time.
  1. Click in the top navigation.
  2. Select to connect to a local AgentOS.
  3. Enter your endpoint URL (default: ).
  4. Name it something like “Development OS”.

You’ll see your OS with a live status indicator once connected. Open Chat, select your agent, and ask: The agent retrieves context from the Agno MCP server and responds with grounded answers.
Click Sessions in the sidebar to inspect stored conversations.All session data is stored in your local database. No third-party tracing or hosted memory service is required.
In 20 lines, you built:

You can use this exact same architecture for running multi-agent systems in production.
  * [Deploy AgentOS to your cloud →](https://docs.agno.com/deploy/introduction)
  * [Browse 2000+ code examples →](https://docs.agno.com/examples/introduction)

* 





Agents are a stateful control loop around a stateless model. The model reasons and calls tools in a loop, guided by instructions. Add memory, knowledge, storage, human-in-the-loop, and guardrails as needed.
[ Create agents with tools and instructions. ](https://docs.agno.com/agents/building-agents)
[ Execute agents and handle responses. ](https://docs.agno.com/agents/running-agents)
[ Inspect and troubleshoot agent behavior. ](https://docs.agno.com/agents/debugging-agents)  
| Orchestrate agents, teams, and functions through defined steps  |  
| --- |

markdown:
* 





A Team is a collection of agents (or sub-teams) that work together. The team leader delegates tasks to members based on their roles.

```




"You answer questions in English"
"You answer questions in Chinese"


"You coordinate the team members to answer questions in German and Dutch"

"You answer questions in German"
"You answer questions in Dutch"




```

Single agents hit limits fast. Context windows fill up, decision-making gets muddy, debugging becomes impossible. Teams distribute work across specialized agents:  
| Each agent masters one domain instead of being mediocre at everything  |  
| --- |  
| Multiple agents work simultaneously on independent subtasks  |  
| When something breaks, you know exactly which agent to fix  |  
| Add capabilities by adding agents, not rewriting everything  |  
The tradeoff: coordination overhead. Agents need to communicate and share state. Get this wrong and you’ve built a more expensive failure mode.
  * A task requires multiple specialized agents with different tools or expertise
  * A single agent’s context window gets exceeded
  * You want each agent focused on a narrow scope

**Use a single agent when:**
  * The task fits one domain of expertise
  * You’re not sure yet (start simple, add agents when you hit limits)

Agno agents and teams are modular. Message building, session handling, storage, and background managers are separated into dedicated components for separation of coordination logic with the APIs. Pass a function instead of a static list for , , or . Agno calls it at the start of each run, injecting context based on the function's parameter names.  
| Cached by custom key > > . Disable with   |  
| --- |  
| Tools and members return a list or tuple. Knowledge returns a instance or   |  
, , and all accept callable factories. Agents also support callable factories for and . See for examples and for cache key configuration. Team 2.0 introduces to make collaboration styles explicit. Prefer instead of toggling or directly. Mode selection controls how the leader collaborates with members. overrides legacy flags. If is not set, maps to , maps to , otherwise .  
| Decompose work, delegate to members, synthesize results  |  
| --- |  
| Route to a single specialist and return their response directly  |  
| Delegate the same task to all members and synthesize  |  
| Run a task list loop until the goal is complete  |  
Modes are explicit orchestration patterns. Use them to name and swap coordination topologies without changing agent logic. Team runs can pause for human-in-the-loop requirements and continue after approval.
[ Define members, roles, and structure. ](https://docs.agno.com/teams/building-teams)
[ Execute teams and handle responses. ](https://docs.agno.com/teams/running-teams)
[ Inspect and troubleshoot team behavior. ](https://docs.agno.com/teams/debugging-teams)
Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

markdown:
The Agno contains 2000+ production-ready examples that showcase the full breadth of Agno. From simple agents to complex multi-agent systems with memory, knowledge, reasoning, and learning.  
| Agents, teams, workflows, knowledge, RAG  |  
| --- |  
| OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Groq, Mistral, local models  |  
| MCP, search, data, communication, AI/media, dev tools  |  
| PostgreSQL, SQLite, MongoDB, MySQL, Redis, DynamoDB  |  
| PgVector, Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Chroma  |  
| Your first agents with storage, memory, knowledge, guardrails, HITL and more.  |  
| --- |  
| Agent patterns: tools, storage, memory, knowledge, learnings, guardrails, multimodal, learning, HITL, hooks, skills and more.  |  
| --- |  
| Multi-agent coordination: modes, structured I/O, human-in-the-loop, knowledge, guardrails, dependencies and more.  |  
| Workflow orchestration: sequential, conditional, parallel, loops, branching, and CEL.  |  
| Database backends: PostgreSQL, MongoDB, SQLite, MySQL, Redis.  |  
| --- |  
| Vector databases, embedders, readers, chunking, filters, and search.  |  
| User memory persistence across runs, sessions, and agents.  |  
| Agents that learn, adapt, and improve from interactions.  |  
| 40+ LLM providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google and more.  |  
| --- |  
| 100+ built-in tools: MCP, custom tools, search, data, communication, AI/media.  |  
| --- |  
| AgentOS: clients, interfaces, databases, middleware, scheduling, and tracing.  |  
| --- |  
| Chain-of-thought reasoning with agents, models, tools, and teams.  |  
| Evaluation patterns: accuracy, agent-as-judge, performance, and reliability.  |  
| External platforms: observability, RAG integrations, Discord, and more.  |  
| Save and load agents, teams, and workflows to/from databases.  |

markdown:
Agno is the runtime for agentic software. Build agents, teams, and workflows. Run them as scalable services. Monitor and manage them in production.  
| Build agents, teams, and workflows with memory, knowledge, guardrails, and 100+ integrations.  |  
| --- |  
| Serve your system in production with a stateless, session-scoped FastAPI backend.  |  
| Test, monitor, and manage your system using the .  |  
Agno powers real agentic systems built from the same primitives above.
  * A personal agent that learns your preferences.
  * A self-learning data agent grounded in six layers of context.
  * A self-learning context agent that manages enterprise context knowledge.
  * A post-IDE coding agent that improves over time.
  * A multi-agent investment committee that debates and allocates capital.

Single agents. Coordinated teams. Structured workflows. All built on one architecture. Agno is designed to scale with you.
  * Build your first agent with memory, knowledge, and tools.
  * Build intelligent programs that reason, use tools, and take action.
  * Coordinate multiple agents to collaborate and reach decisions.
  * Orchestrate deterministic and agentic steps into structured systems.
  * Deploy, govern, and operate agents in production.

Start small with a single agent. Or build distributed, governed multi-agent systems from day one. Agno runs in your infrastructure, not ours.
  * 50+ APIs and background execution.
  * Native tracing and full auditability.
  * Sessions, memory, knowledge, and traces stored in your database.

You own the system. You own the data. You define the rules.
Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

Get the messages for the given session ID.
  * (Optional[str]): The session ID to get the messages for. If not provided, the latest used session ID is used.
  * (Optional[int]): The number of runs to return messages from, counting from the latest. Defaults to all runs.
  * (Optional[int]): The number of messages to return, counting from the latest. Defaults to all messages.

  * (List[Union[Toolkit, Callable, Function, Dict]]): The tools to set


Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

**AgentOS turns your agents into a production service you can deploy anywhere.**
  * : 50+ ready to use endpoints with SSE-compatible streaming.
  * : Sessions, memory, knowledge, and traces stored in your database.
  * : No state bleed between users, agents, or sessions.
  * : JWT-based RBAC with hierarchical scopes.
  * : Traces stored in your database with no third-party egress or vendor lock-in.
  * : Guardrails, human-in-the-loop, and approval flows for full control.

Here’s a minimal example of the AgentOS serving an Agent with memory, state and MCP: AgentOS consists of two parts:
  1. : Runs agents built with the Agno SDK (using FastAPI).
  2. : UI for managing, monitoring, and debugging your system.

The runtime exposes a set of APIs that powers both, the control plane and your AI products. The AgentOS runs as a container in your cloud. The UI connects directly from the browser. No proxies. No data relays. Your data stays completely private. Most AI tooling stores your data on their servers. You pay retention costs, deal with egress fees, and depend on their security. AgentOS runs entirely in your infrastructure:
  * : Sessions, memory, knowledge, traces. All stored where you control it.
  * : No conversations, logs, or metrics sent to Agno.
  * : The control plane connects from your browser to the runtime. Agno stores no data except for your runtime endpoint. All data resides in your database.


[ Initialize and serve a basic agent. ](https://docs.agno.com/agent-os/run-your-os)
[ Manage AgentOS using a Web UI. ](https://docs.agno.com/agent-os/connect-your-os)
[ Inspect sessions and debug traces. ](https://docs.agno.com/agent-os/control-plane)
[ ](https://docs.agno.com/agent-os/overview)
Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

metadata.description: The production runtime and control plane for multi-agent systems.

markdown:
Go from zero to production in three steps: deploy, customize, and connect.
Start here. Clone a deployment template to deploy AgentOS in your environment.
[ Run AgentOS locally using Docker. ](https://docs.agno.com/production/templates/docker)
[Browse all deployment templates →](https://docs.agno.com/production/templates/overview)
Each application is complete with production-ready agent, team, or workflow. Adapt it to your use case and run in your AgentOS deployment.
[ Query databases with natural language. ](https://docs.agno.com/production/applications/text-to-sql)
[ ](https://docs.agno.com/production/applications/research-agent)
[ Answer questions from your knowledge base. ](https://docs.agno.com/production/applications/knowledge-agent)
Connect your applications to the platforms your users already use. Expose your applications via Slack, Discord, MCP, or a custom UI.
[ Expose agents as Slack apps. ](https://docs.agno.com/production)
[ Expose agents as Discord bots. ](https://docs.agno.com/production)
[ Expose agents as an MCP server. ](https://docs.agno.com/production)
[Browse all supported interfaces →](https://docs.agno.com/production/interfaces/overview)
Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.
